One Link
How it works Features Security Share Get One Link
How it works

One Link in four steps.

No setup wizard. No questionnaire. The four steps below already happened the moment you opened the app for the first time. You did not see any of it.

1. Open the app.

That is the whole signup. No email. No phone. No password. Your device generates a key the first time. The key is your identity. It never leaves the device.

2. Pair with someone.

Show them a QR. They scan it. You both see the same five words. You both say "yes." You are connected. The whole thing takes five seconds. The five words mean the math agreed.

3. Send something.

Drag a file. Type a message. Drop a folder. It goes straight from your device to theirs. Encrypted before it leaves. Verified when it arrives. Nothing in the middle.

4. Done.

That was it. No "syncing." No "uploaded to your account." No "we will email you a link." It happened. It is on their device. It is not anywhere else.

If they are offline

The network waits.

If the person you are sending to is asleep or off the grid, a volunteer relay holds your encrypted bundle until they come back. The relay cannot read it. The relay does not know who sent it. The relay forgets it once delivered.

Encrypted before it left.

The relay sees a sealed box and a recipient identifier. Nothing else. The contents are unreadable without the recipient's key.

Held briefly.

Up to seven days. After that, gone. Most messages and files clear within seconds because both devices are usually awake.

Many relays, never one.

Anyone can run a relay. The network picks the healthiest one near each side automatically. If a relay disappears, traffic moves to another silently.

If you want to hide your trail

Tap one toggle. Three hops.

Private mode routes your traffic through three volunteer nodes on the way to the recipient. Nobody, including us, can see who sent what to whom. Off by default for speed. On when you need it.

Each hop only knows the hop before and the hop after. The first hop sees you but not the message. The last hop sees the destination but not who you are. No hop in the middle sees either.

This is the same idea Tor uses, with two extra things: it uses post-quantum keys, and it binds each hop to the surrounding physical context so a stolen hop key cannot be replayed elsewhere.

// you tap "private"
pick three healthy relays
wrap message in three layers
layer 1  only the first relay can peel
layer 2  only the second relay can peel
layer 3  only the destination can read

send  no single hop sees both ends
The math if you want it

Everything else is on this page.

If you do not care about the cryptography, stop reading and go pair a device. If you do care, every primitive One Link uses is named below with the crate that implements it. All open source. All audited.

The full cryptographic stack
identity
Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65 hybrid signature. Your device key signs everything you do.
session keys
X25519 today, with the ML-KEM-768 hybrid slot reserved in the wire format. Sphinx onion hops use the hybrid live; pair and channel handshakes activate it in v0.22.
forward secrecy
Double Ratchet (Signal-style) over the hybrid root.
per-chunk crypto
ChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD, rekey every N chunks.
pairing verification
5-word SAS derived from the shared secret. Levenshtein-audited word list.
onion routing
Sphinx Coherence (Ristretto255 + filler-byte construction + PQ-hybrid blinding + field witness).
signature aggregation
Schnorr aggregation over Ristretto255 with Pippenger MSM batch verify.
confidential build
ol_confidential AttestationDoc with PQ-hybrid, peer nonce, 30s freshness.
threshold recovery
BN multi-sig with per-signer R values, k-of-n share split.
routing
tau_c coherence field (ol_coherence_field Helmholtz solver) ranks chunk holders today. Full multi-hop field-routed delivery activates with the relay-mesh cutover in v0.22.
capability access
Macaroon-based caps with constant-time verification and 1M-iter soundness gate.
storage
ChunkRatchet in transit. Zeroize on drop. At-rest encryption is opt-in today via the ONE_LINK_PASSPHRASE wrap on the identity key; the PWA tier ships always-on at-rest encryption in v0.22.
Get One Link → Read the protocol
One Link

A free, private network for messages, files, and devices. Owned by no one. Belongs to everyone.
